events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African descent, a retired high school science teacher, nonsmoker, with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 10 years prior, on metformin), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. No family history of pancreatic cancer. BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

At age 66, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, new-onset weight loss of approximately 14 lbs, and new-onset diabetes worsening (previously well-controlled on metformin, now with fasting glucoses in the 220-280 mg/dL range despite medication adjustment). She also reported early satiety and intermittent nausea. Physical exam revealed mild epigastric tenderness without jaundice. Labs notable for CA 19-9 of 1,200 U/mL, CEA 5.8 ng/mL, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/mL, ALT 54 U/mL, AST 48 U/mL. HbA1c 9.2%. She was referred urgently for cross-sectional imaging.

At age 66, the patient underwent a CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (triphasic with thin cuts). Findings: a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with poorly defined margins. Tumor contacts greater than 180 degrees of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over a length of approximately 1.8 cm. Tumor abuts the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with narrowing of the SMV lumen by approximately 40% over a 2.1 cm segment; no occlusion. No involvement of the portal vein confluence. No involvement of the celiac axis or hepatic artery. No radiographically apparent lymph node metastases (nodes measuring up to 9 mm). No liver lesions or peritoneal implants detected. No ascites. Impression: Borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma by NCCN criteria (SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV abutment with lumen narrowing). Cancer present. No distant metastasis identified.

At age 66, the patient underwent a PET-CT (18F-FDG) for staging purposes. Findings: hypermetabolic activity in the pancreatic head/uncinate mass (SUVmax 9.2) consistent with the known primary tumor. No FDG-avid hepatic lesions. No FDG-avid peritoneal deposits. No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes in the peripancreatic, mesenteric, or retroperitoneal regions. No distant metastatic disease identified. Impression: Locally advanced disease without evidence of distant metastasis. Cancer present.

At age 66, the patient underwent an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy (FNB) of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology report: Specimen type: EUS-FNB, pancreatic head. Diagnosis: ADENOCARCINOMA, PANCREATIC DUCTAL TYPE (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY). Microscopic description: Atypical glandular structures embedded in a desmoplastic stroma with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, irregular nuclear contours, prominent nucleoli, and infiltrative growth pattern. Mucin production present. Features consistent with moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, MUC1 positive, MUC5AC focally positive, CDX2 negative, synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative, p53 aberrant (strong diffuse overexpression pattern). Smad4/DPC4 retained (positive) staining. Ki-67 proliferative index approximately 30%.

At age 66, the patient received a diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process. AJCC 8th Edition Clinical Stage: T3 N0 M0, Stage IIA (by clinical staging based on cross-sectional imaging). Summary Stage: Regional (borderline resectable by NCCN criteria due to SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV abutment with lumen narrowing). Primary site: Pancreatic head/uncinate process (ICD-O-3 site code C25.0). Histology: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated (ICD-O-3 histology code 8500/3). Tumor size: 3.4 cm. Relevant biomarkers: CA 19-9 1,200 U/mL at diagnosis; CEA 5.8 ng/mL. Smad4/DPC4 retained. p53 aberrant overexpression. Grade: G2 (moderately differentiated). Confirmation: Positive histology via EUS-FNB. Laterality: Midline/head of pancreas.

At age 66, the patient underwent next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT solid tumor panel) performed on tissue from the EUS-FNB specimen of the pancreatic head mass. Results: Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 3 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: Microsatellite stable (MSS). Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: KRAS p.G12V (variant allele frequency 42%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver, no approved targeted therapy; RAS inhibitors in development). TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%, pathogenic, loss-of-function, consistent with IHC overexpression pattern). CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss, tumor suppressor, no targeted therapy). SMAD4 wild-type (consistent with IHC). Additional findings: ARID1A p.Q1368* (VAF 11%, likely pathogenic, chromatin remodeling). RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (VAF 28%, pathogenic, WNT pathway). KDM6A deep deletion (copy number loss). ATM wild-type. BRCA1 wild-type. BRCA2 wild-type. PALB2 wild-type. FGFR2 wild-type. No NTRK fusions detected. No ALK/ROS1 fusions detected. No NRG1 fusions detected. No actionable fusions or gene amplifications identified. Germline testing recommended given pancreatic cancer diagnosis; patient subsequently had germline testing which showed no pathogenic germline variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, or MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2.

At age 66, the patient was enrolled in a multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board discussion and the consensus recommendation was neoadjuvant chemotherapy with modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) for borderline resectable disease with the intent of tumor downstaging, followed by restaging and consideration for chemoradiation and then surgical resection. Cycle 1 of mFOLFIRINOX was initiated: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV day 1 (reduced from 180 mg/m2 given patient age and diabetes), 5-fluorouracil 2,400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours; every 2 weeks. Supportive medications: granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) filgrastim prophylaxis, ondansetron, prochlorperazine, and loperamide as needed. Intent: neoadjuvant/curative.

At age 66, during cycles 2-4 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (paresthesias in fingertips and toes, worsened by cold exposure), grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 nausea (partially controlled with antiemetics), and grade 1 alopecia. She also developed grade 2 diarrhea during cycle 3, managed with loperamide and dose reduction of irinotecan to 120 mg/m2 for subsequent cycles. No febrile neutropenia. No hospitalizations required. ECOG performance status remained 1 throughout.

At age 66, after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 2 months into treatment), the patient underwent restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol. Findings: The pancreatic head/uncinate mass measures 3.0 cm (previously 3.4 cm), representing a minor decrease in size. Persistence of SMA abutment, now measuring approximately 160 degrees of contact (previously >180 degrees), slight improvement. SMV narrowing persists but appears slightly less severe. No new liver lesions. No new peritoneal deposits. No new lymphadenopathy. CA 19-9 at this restaging: 380 U/mL (down from 1,200 U/mL at baseline). Impression: Mild partial radiographic response per modified RECIST criteria. Cancer present. Responding to therapy. Recommendation: Continue mFOLFIRINOX toward planned 8 cycles.

At age 66, at the midpoint restaging visit after 4 cycles, the oncologist noted continued CA 19-9 decline (1,200 to 380 U/mL), mild radiographic response, and improvement in back pain. Patient's weight had stabilized. Nausea and fatigue were the predominant side effects. The plan was to continue mFOLFIRINOX for an additional 4 cycles (cycles 5-8), then perform definitive restaging. Performance status ECOG 1. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, after completing 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 4 months after treatment initiation), the patient underwent definitive restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver. CT findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 2.6 cm (previously 3.0 cm at interim restaging, 3.4 cm at baseline), representing a partial response by RECIST 1.1 (>30% reduction in longest diameter from baseline). SMA abutment has decreased to approximately 120 degrees of contact (previously >180 degrees at baseline). SMV lumen narrowing has improved with now only mild abutment without significant luminal compromise. MRI liver: No hepatic metastases identified. No peritoneal implants. No new lymphadenopathy. Impression: Partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. Improved but persistent SMA abutment (120 degrees). Improved SMV relationship. Case to be re-presented at multidisciplinary tumor board. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, following completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX, CA 19-9 declined to 85 U/mL (from 1,200 U/mL at baseline; 93% reduction). Patient re-presented at multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Surgical oncologist noted improved but persistent SMA abutment at 120 degrees, which remained technically borderline. Consensus decision: proceed with chemoradiation (capecitabine-based long-course) prior to surgical exploration, with the goal of further local consolidation and improving the likelihood of R0 resection. Patient counseled on the plan. ECOG performance status 1. No evidence of distant metastasis. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, approximately 4.5 months after diagnosis, the patient received concurrent chemoradiation therapy (long-course). Radiation: stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT)-based approach was considered but conventional long-course selected given degree of vascular abutment. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) delivered via intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the pancreatic head tumor with a 5 mm clinical target volume margin. Concurrent chemotherapy: capecitabine 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days. Radiation field included primary tumor with margin; prophylactic nodal irradiation was not performed. Treatment completed over approximately 5.5 weeks without treatment breaks. Intent: neoadjuvant/curative, local consolidation prior to surgery.

At age 66, during chemoradiation, the patient experienced grade 2 nausea and vomiting (managed with ondansetron and metoclopramide), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 radiation dermatitis overlying the epigastric region, and grade 1 diarrhea (managed conservatively). She required a 3-day treatment break in week 4 due to grade 2 nausea with poor oral intake. Blood glucose remained elevated during radiation, requiring insulin dose adjustment. No grade 3 or higher toxicities. Weight loss of 4 lbs during chemoradiation. ECOG performance status 1-2 during treatment, returning to 1 by end of chemoradiation.

At age 66, approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation, the patient underwent a restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol prior to surgical planning. Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass measures 2.3 cm (previously 2.6 cm after mFOLFIRINOX). Persistent soft tissue density in the region consistent with treated tumor versus fibrosis, difficult to distinguish post-treatment changes. SMA abutment now estimated at 80-90 degrees of contact. SMV relationship: abutment without significant luminal narrowing. No liver metastases. No peritoneal deposits. No lymphadenopathy. CA 19-9 at this time: 52 U/mL. Impression: Continued response to neoadjuvant therapy. Reduced SMA abutment to <90 degrees. Potentially resectable. Surgical oncology consultation for Whipple procedure planning. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, approximately 8 weeks after completing chemoradiation (approximately 7 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent exploratory laparotomy and pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed by an experienced hepatopancreaticobiliary (HPB) surgical oncologist at a high-volume center. Operative findings: No peritoneal metastases. No liver surface metastases. Frozen section of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) margin: negative for malignancy. Intraoperative ultrasound confirmed no liver lesions. The procedure included: pancreaticoduodenectomy with pylorus-preserving technique, regional lymphadenectomy (stations 5, 6, 8a, 12b, 13, 14, 17), portal vein/SMV reconstruction was not required as the vessel was freed with careful dissection. Total operative time: 6 hours 45 minutes. Estimated blood loss: 450 mL. No intraoperative transfusions required. Patient transferred to surgical ICU postoperatively in stable condition.

At age 66, final surgical pathology from the Whipple (pancreaticoduodenectomy) specimen was reported. Specimen: Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy. Diagnosis: PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (Grade 2). Tumor location: Pancreatic head/uncinate process. Tumor size: 2.1 cm (greatest dimension; reduced from 3.4 cm at diagnosis, representing significant treatment response). Histologic treatment response (College of American Pathologists grading): Grade 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident tumor regression, estimated 40% viable tumor cells remaining, 60% fibrosis/acellular mucin pools). Margins: Surgical margins negative for invasive carcinoma (R0 resection). Margin distances: Posterior/retroperitoneal margin: 2 mm. Uncinate/SMA margin: 3 mm (closest margin). Anterior surface: 5 mm. Bile duct margin: negative (>10 mm). Pancreatic neck margin: negative (>10 mm). Duodenal margins (proximal and distal): negative. Lymph nodes: 2 of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (2/18; pN1). Largest metastatic deposit: 4 mm. No extranodal extension. Lymphovascular invasion: Present. Perineural invasion: Present (extensive). Pathologic staging (AJCC 8th Edition): pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB. SMAD4/DPC4 immunohistochemistry on resection specimen: retained (consistent with prior biopsy).

At age 66, at the post-operative visit 4 weeks after Whipple procedure, the patient was recovering from surgery. She developed a grade B pancreatic fistula (International Study Group of Pancreatic Fistula [ISGPF] grade B) on postoperative day 6, as evidenced by elevated amylase in drain fluid (drain fluid amylase >3x upper limit of normal on postoperative day 3). This was managed conservatively with continued surgical drain in place, NPO (nothing by mouth), total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. The drain was kept in place for 6 weeks until drain output decreased and amylase normalized. No surgical re-intervention was required. Additional postoperative complications: delayed gastric emptying (grade A, resolved by postoperative day 14 with nasogastric tube decompression). The patient was discharged home on postoperative day 18 with drain in place. Pathologic staging: pT3 pN1 M0 (Stage IIB). R0 resection confirmed. Cancer: post-resection status, no gross residual disease.

At age 66, approximately 10 weeks after the Whipple procedure (after pancreatic fistula resolved and prior to initiating adjuvant chemotherapy), the patient underwent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast for baseline post-surgical imaging. Findings: Post-pancreaticoduodenectomy changes with expected anatomical alterations (hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, gastrojejunostomy). No fluid collections or abscesses. Surgical drain has been removed (externally). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No lymphadenopathy meeting criteria for malignancy. CA 19-9: 28 U/mL (normalized; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL). CEA: 2.1 ng/mL. Impression: Expected post-surgical changes without evidence of residual or recurrent disease. No cancer identified on this study.

At age 66, approximately 12 weeks after the Whipple procedure (following resolution of the grade B pancreatic fistula and recovery), the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen): gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle plus capecitabine 1,660 mg/m2/day (830 mg/m2 twice daily) orally on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle. Total planned: 6 cycles (approximately 6 months). ECOG performance status 1. Intent: adjuvant/curative.

At age 66, during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, the patient experienced the following toxicities: Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) beginning cycle 2, managed with dose reduction of capecitabine to 75% (1,245 mg/m2/day) after cycle 2. Grade 2 fatigue throughout treatment. Grade 1 myelosuppression (mild anemia; nadir hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, managed with iron supplementation). Grade 1 nausea. Day 8 gemcitabine omitted in cycles 3 and 5 due to neutropenia (ANC <1.0 x 10^9/L). Re-hospitalization on cycle 3 day 12 for a suspected pancreatic fistula recurrence: the patient presented with abdominal pain and elevated serum amylase/lipase. CT abdomen confirmed a small peripancreatic fluid collection (4 cm); managed conservatively with IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), bowel rest, and TPN. No percutaneous drainage required. Collection resolved within 2 weeks. Chemotherapy held for 3 weeks during this hospitalization, then resumed with capecitabine dose reduction to 50% for cycle 4. Despite dose modifications and treatment delays, all 6 cycles were ultimately completed over approximately 9 months (rather than the planned 6 months).

At age 66 (during adjuvant therapy), the oncologist noted that the patient was tolerating adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine with significant dose modifications and delays secondary to recurrent pancreatic fistula and hand-foot syndrome. Despite these complications, she was able to complete all 6 planned cycles. CA 19-9 remained within normal range throughout adjuvant therapy (nadir 18 U/mL). ECOG performance status 1. Patient continued on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT; pancrelipase) and insulin for post-pancreatectomy exocrine and endocrine insufficiency. No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 3 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 18 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No pulmonary nodules or masses suspicious for metastasis. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Post-surgical anatomy unchanged from prior study. CA 19-9: 22 U/mL (within normal limits). CEA: 2.4 ng/mL. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 6 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 21 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No pulmonary metastases. Post-surgical anatomy stable. Mild peripancreatic fibrosis at the anastomotic site, unchanged. CA 19-9: 19 U/mL. CEA: 2.2 ng/mL. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 12 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 27 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: New 1.2 cm hypodense hepatic lesion in segment VI, indeterminate but suspicious given oncologic history. Additionally, two new small indeterminate pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 7 mm and left lower lobe 5 mm (previously absent). No peritoneal implants. CA 19-9: 67 U/mL (rising above upper limit of normal of 37 U/mL). CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (rising). Impression: New indeterminate hepatic lesion and pulmonary nodules in the context of rising CA 19-9 and CEA, highly suspicious for metastatic recurrence. Further characterization recommended. Cancer present (suspected).

At age 67, following the suspicious surveillance CT, the patient underwent MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) contrast for further characterization of the hepatic lesion. Findings: The 1.2 cm segment VI hepatic lesion demonstrates arterial phase enhancement with portal venous washout and hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase images. Features are highly characteristic of metastatic adenocarcinoma rather than hepatocellular carcinoma. No additional hepatic lesions identified. Impression: Hepatic segment VI lesion consistent with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Cancer present.

At age 67, the patient underwent CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the segment VI hepatic lesion. Pathology: Specimen: Core needle biopsy, liver, segment VI. Diagnosis: METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH PANCREATIC PRIMARY (MALIGNANT). Microscopic description: Infiltrative glandular structures with desmoplastic stroma, moderate-to-severe nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and intraluminal mucin, identical in morphology to the prior pancreatic primary. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative, PAX8 negative, MUC1 positive, SMAD4/DPC4 retained. Findings confirm metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

At age 67, the patient was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board following biopsy-confirmed hepatic metastasis (segment VI), two indeterminate pulmonary nodules (likely metastatic given the hepatic confirmation), and rising CA 19-9 (67 U/mL) and CEA (4.8 ng/mL). Pathologic stage now M1 (Stage IV metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma). The patient had recurred approximately 12 months after completing adjuvant therapy (approximately 27 months from initial diagnosis). Disease-free interval: approximately 20 months from surgery. ECOG performance status 1. Prior therapies: mFOLFIRINOX (8 cycles neoadjuvant), capecitabine-based chemoradiation, gemcitabine/capecitabine (6 cycles adjuvant). Molecular profile reviewed: KRAS G12V mutant, MSS, TMB-low, SMAD4 retained, no actionable fusions, no BRCA/ATM/PALB2 mutations. Plan: systemic therapy for metastatic disease. Given prior FOLFIRINOX exposure and >12 month interval from last chemotherapy, discussed options including gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, re-challenge with FOLFIRINOX, or clinical trial enrollment. Patient elected to pursue gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel given prior oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy and convenience. Cancer present, disease progression (new metastatic recurrence).

At age 67, the patient initiated first-line palliative systemic therapy for metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma with gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV plus nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle. Concurrent supportive care: ondansetron, dexamethasone premedication for nab-paclitaxel, G-CSF per institutional protocol. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy and insulin continued. Palliative care consultation initiated. Intent: palliative.

At age 67, during palliative gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (additive to residual oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy from prior FOLFIRINOX), grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 alopecia, and grade 2 neutropenia requiring dose reduction of nab-paclitaxel to 100 mg/m2 after cycle 2. Day 8 and day 15 doses were held once each in cycles 3 and 4 due to neutropenia. No febrile neutropenia. Grade 1 nausea throughout. Blood glucose remained elevated, requiring insulin dose adjustments.

At age 67, after 3 cycles of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Findings: The hepatic segment VI lesion is stable at 1.2 cm. Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule stable at 7 mm; left lower lobe nodule stable at 5 mm. No new lesions. No peritoneal implants. CA 19-9: 45 U/mL (declining from 67 U/mL). CEA: 3.9 ng/mL. Impression: Stable disease by RECIST 1.1. CA 19-9 trending down. Cancer present. Responding (stable disease) to therapy.

At age 67, following 3 cycles of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel with stable disease and declining CA 19-9, the oncology team discussed continuation of therapy. The patient expressed goals of care focused on maintaining quality of life and functional independence. She remained ECOG performance status 1 with manageable toxicities. Plan: continue gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel with dose modifications as needed. Palliative care team co-managing symptom burden. Advance directives updated. Cancer present, stable disease on palliative therapy.

scenario_blurb: Locally advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma (borderline resectable) in a 66-year-old with tumor abutting the SMA and SMV. CA 19-9 elevated to 1200. Treated with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with CA 19-9 decline to 85 and partial response on imaging. Then chemoradiation with capecitabine. Successfully undergoes Whipple procedure with negative margins. Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine complicated by pancreatic fistula.
